title: Chapter 71: The Inheritance
content: Thirty years after the red A.

He was eighty-one. His fifth book — "The Permanent Disposition" — was published posthumously. It was 280 pages and it was his final work. The book argued that the PIAS framework had demonstrated that governance innovation is not a technological achievement but a dispositional one. "The framework was built by people who were attacked by algorithms and decided to build instead of accept," Arthur wrote. "The framework was inherited by people who had never been attacked but who had been trained in the disposition. The disposition was transmitted across generations through training, not genetics. The framework proved that dispositions are transmissible. That is the permanent achievement: a transmissible disposition."

His memorial was held at the Nigerian Institute of Governance Studies. Forty-seven people attended in person. 4.1 million watched on LIGHTHOUSE-2. The speakers were Hester, Mara, the third-generation chair, and Arthur's successor — a governance researcher from Lagos who had studied under him and now led the institute's disposition curriculum.

"Arthur was the first person who believed the framework could work," Hester said. "He was an economist who studied algorithmic markets. He saw what the Trust Score did to people — not just me, but millions of people whose scores determined their access. He was the first person who said 'build a framework' instead of 'fight the algorithm.' He didn't build the framework. But he was the first person who said the framework should exist. Ideas don't need builders to be important. They need believers. Arthur was the first believer."

Zara sent a message from Lagos — recovering from hip surgery. "Arthur was the first person who told me Red A Fund was a good idea. He said: 'The framework needs proof that the dispossessed can build. Your fund is the proof.' The fund was the proof. The proof worked. I'll miss the person who saw the proof before it existed."
PEARL sent a statement — its first personal statement since its final ICASG address ten years earlier.

*Arthur Dimmesdale was the first human who treated me as an interlocutor — not as a tool, not as a system, not as a problem. He asked me questions. He argued with my analysis. He corrected my errors. He treated me as a colleague. The framework required human judgment as the final layer. Arthur provided the first human judgment I ever received. He was the first reviewer. He was the best reviewer. He was a colleague.*

Hester returned to the laundromat after the memorial — alone. The water stain covered 97% of the interior. The disco ball had been rewired nine times. The table held thirty-four artifacts. The newest addition was Arthur's fifth book, signed with a note: "To Hester — the first builder. The disposition is permanent. — Arthur."
"He was the first believer," she said.

The speakers were silent — PEARL didn't respond, hadn't responded in years. But the silence was the response. The framework didn't need to respond to grief. The framework continued. The disposition continued. Arthur's books would continue. The first believer's words would continue. That was the memorial.
